text: Since the deployment of Provincial Reconstruction Team (PRT) personnel to Afghanistan, NGOs have voiced a number of concerns related to the blending of military and humanitarian aid activities. Chief among these is the assertion that the activities conducted by the PRTs and the US military have obviated any distinction between combatants and humanitarian aid workers, thereby increasing the likelihood of attack by insurgents. Though widely referenced in policy and media statements, this assertion has not been subject to rigorous empirical scrutiny. The purpose of this paper is to empirically investigate the impact of PRT deployments on the environment in which aid workers operate. Using a panel-corrected standard error regression model applied to data across all 34 provinces of Afghanistan for calendar years 2010 and 2011, the study concludes that the number of incidents reported by NGOs were higher in provinces with a PRT presence. However, a detailed examination of the coefficients reveals that these incidents were largely confined to those provinces that were commanded by foreign forces (i.e. not US troops). Hence, the commonly referenced "blurred lines" assertion, which has been used to explain increased attacks against aid workers, does not universally apply.
